session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Kaja Kallas
extraction: The missing element in the international order is accountability: the rules exist and most countries sign up to the UN Charter, but there is no functioning mechanism to hold violators accountable, especially when powerful states breach the principles.
context: Referenced the progression from the League of Nations to the UN, arguing each crisis led to further development of international law. Pointed to the Security Council as the main accountability mechanism that is not working.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Prince Faisal bin Farhan Al Saud
extraction: The panic about the breakdown of the rules-based order is a Eurocentric awakening; much of the developing world has lived with might-makes-right for over a decade, and the current conversation is finally catching up to their reality.
context: Responded to the moderator's suggestion that people in his region see the old order's collapse as a transatlantic-bubble phenomenon. Cited 2014 as an earlier wake-up call that Europe failed to heed.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Prince Faisal bin Farhan Al Saud
extraction: Might-makes-right has become more salient globally not because it is new but because it now visibly affects the great powers themselves, whereas historically its consequences were confined to the developing world.
context: Argued that Europe and the US had the luxury of being at a remove. The war in Ukraine brought the principle home to them in a way that conflicts in the Middle East and Global South had not.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Prince Faisal bin Farhan Al Saud
extraction: He is more optimistic than a year ago because for the first time even Europe's most ardent supporters of the old system are admitting it is broken, enabling honest conversation about what replaces it.
context: Said European attachment to 'symbolism rather than realities on the ground' had previously been an obstacle to substantive discussions about conflict drivers.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Pedro Arnulfo Sánchez Suárez
extraction: The new US national security strategy's focus on the Americas is a 'fantastic opportunity' for Colombia, because US strategic attention has historically neglected the Western Hemisphere in favor of Russia, China, and the Middle East.
context: Described studying at Edwards College in Alabama in 2018 and always asking why the American continent didn't figure in US national security strategy. Referenced the December strategy refocusing on the hemisphere. [word uncertain in source]

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Pedro Arnulfo Sánchez Suárez
extraction: Misinformation and disinformation led President Trump to believe Colombia was doing nothing to fight drug trafficking; the Trump-Petro meeting corrected this and showed that every country, no matter how powerful, is vulnerable to this scourge.
context: Cited Global Risk ranking disinformation as the second-greatest global risk after interstate conflicts, predicted it could become the greatest due to AI. Noted 316 million global drug consumers and 2,000+ Colombian military/police casualties in 3.5 years of counter-narcotics. [word uncertain in source]

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Pedro Arnulfo Sánchez Suárez
extraction: Colombia reframes 'America First' as 'Americas First' — hemispheric cooperation rather than US dominance — arguing that shared threats like transnational organized crime can only be addressed collectively.
context: Described President Petro adding an 'S' to the cap President Trump gave him to symbolize continental partnership. Argued drug trafficking is an economic problem requiring demand-side solutions, not just weapons.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Michael Waltz
extraction: US reassertion of leadership in the Western Hemisphere is overdue, not a new departure; the results — Maduro removed, a new UN-mandated force in Haiti, 8 million Venezuelan refugees potentially returning — have been privately welcomed by countries in the region.
context: Referenced Plan Colombia in the 1990s-2000s as precedent. Claimed behind-the-scenes thanks from regional countries, 7 of whom would meet with the president in coming weeks. Cited the Venezuelan refugee crisis as larger than Syria's.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Michael Waltz
extraction: The UN has been trying to do too much for everyone and needs to go back to basics — its core peacemaking and peacekeeping function — which requires significant budget and personnel cuts.
context: Cited the UN's first-ever budget cut of 15%, nearly 3,000 headquarters bureaucrats to be cut, 18% overall personnel reduction, 25% peacekeeping cut. Claimed humanitarian agencies in Geneva privately encouraged him to keep up the pressure.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Michael Waltz
extraction: Trump will do for the UN what he has done for NATO over the last ten years — use sustained pressure to force reforms that make the institution stronger and fit for purpose.
context: Drew an analogy to NATO, where fewer than 7 members met the spending minimum a decade ago. Argued that despite criticism, NATO is now in a better place because of US pressure on contributions.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Michael Waltz
extraction: 'Focused multilateralism' — ad hoc plurilateral coalitions that deliver concrete results — should be acceptable as a complement or alternative to traditional universal institutions when those institutions fail to resolve conflicts.
context: Cited the Board of Peace, the Critical Minerals Conference, the first direct flight between Azerbaijan and Armenia, and wheat shipments through the Caucasus as examples of focused multilateral results. Also referenced the Abraham Accords grouping and Pakistan-India engagement.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Michael Waltz
extraction: The world was on the brink 18 months ago — Ukraine, Gaza, Iran racing for a nuke, Houthis shutting down the Suez Canal, the South American refugee crisis — and the current administration is pulling it back.
context: Pushed back on the panel's framing ('The World on the Brink') by arguing the brink was the prior situation and the current trajectory is improvement.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Kaja Kallas
extraction: The Board of Peace charter diverges from UN Security Council Resolution 2803: the resolution limited the Board's mandate in time until 2027, required Palestinian participation, and was specifically about Gaza, whereas the White House charter addresses none of these.
context: Responded directly to Waltz's defense of the Board of Peace as a UNSC-blessed institution. Argued the gap between the resolution text and the charter undermines the claim of multilateral legitimacy.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Kaja Kallas
extraction: In any reformed multilateral system, equality of states must be preserved and no one — including the most powerful countries — should be above the law; the Board of Peace charter, where one person can make decisions without taking others' views into account, violates this principle.
context: Argued that if somebody breaches agreed international rules, 'even the most powerful countries,' there must be accountability. Drew contrast between partnerships based on dignity and structures where one actor dominates decision-making.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Michael Waltz
extraction: The US has historically paid more than 180 countries combined in UN dues and voluntary contributions; it is fair for American taxpayers — struggling mechanics, doctors, nurses — to demand that their money is well spent rather than funding UN officials flying business class.
context: Responded to Kallas's equality-of-states argument by shifting the frame to financial burden-sharing. Referenced his former constituents in Indiana, Georgia, and North Florida.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Kaja Kallas
extraction: What distinguishes the United States from Russia and China as a superpower is that it has allies willing to fight alongside it; undermining those alliances by treating partners as subordinates would eliminate America's core strategic advantage.
context: Pointed out that when Russia goes to war it goes alone because it has no allies, while when America goes to war allies join and lose their people. Argued that against China's economic and military might, allies are the differentiator.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Kaja Kallas
extraction: Europe is not free-riding: it provides the largest share of global development aid, and the US argument that Europeans contribute nothing is inaccurate.
context: Direct rebuttal to Waltz's framing of European under-contribution. Argued that if you look at all UN contributions including development aid, Europe's share is substantial.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Kaja Kallas
extraction: All European countries are small countries — or those that haven't yet realized they are small countries — and therefore inherently need a rules-based order to survive.
context: Framed European support for multilateralism as existential rather than idealistic, given that even the largest European states are small relative to the US, China, or Russia.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Michael Waltz
extraction: The international system hasn't been 'coming unglued now' — it's been coming unglued for 10 years since 2014 with no effective response, and the current administration is simply asking more forcefully for the reforms everyone acknowledged were needed.
context: Pushed back on the suggestion that the current moment represents a new crisis, arguing the deterioration has been long-running and the US is now providing the forcing function that was absent.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Michael Waltz
extraction: Some countries were not comfortable putting their funding entirely through the UN system, so the Board of Peace created a separate funding mechanism — this is a practical response to institutional distrust, not a circumvention of multilateralism.
context: Explained why the Board of Peace has its own funding channel alongside the Palestinian Technocratic Committee. Cited billions of dollars in contributions.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Michael Waltz
extraction: Only President Trump could have brought all sides — including Israel — to the table on Gaza, producing a ceasefire, hostage releases, humanitarian aid at over 4,000 trucks per week, and a Security Council resolution committing to no occupation or annexation and Palestinian self-determination.
context: Referenced the Sharm el-Sheikh meeting, Abu Mazen shaking the president's hand, the Knesset speech, and 8,000 troops contributed by Indonesia for the international security force.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Prince Faisal bin Farhan Al Saud
extraction: It's not a ceasefire if the guns haven't ceased; even if killing has stopped, dying hasn't — aid access remains the critical unresolved issue in Gaza.
context: Acknowledged US engagement on the issue is daily and genuine, but said it doesn't always translate into results on the ground. Noted Saudi Arabia remains engaged because of this gap.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Prince Faisal bin Farhan Al Saud
extraction: Palestinian self-determination must be the endpoint of the Gaza process, and the unity of Gaza and the West Bank must be maintained — there can be no pathway to Palestinian rights if the two territories are severed.
context: Argued that stabilizing Gaza is a precondition for reconnecting it to the West Bank, which in turn is necessary for any viable Palestinian self-determination. Framed this as the Kingdom's firm position.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Pedro Arnulfo Sánchez Suárez
extraction: Multilateral organizations like the UN and OAS remain the appropriate framework for tackling transnational threats; nations are more civilized now and can create a better world through cooperation rather than dominance.
context: Argued that President Petro maintains that a global order and multilateralism exist and should be used. Stated 'we don't have life on other planets' as a reason for working together on this one.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Prince Faisal bin Farhan Al Saud
extraction: The transition period between the old system and whatever replaces it will be more dangerous than either endpoint, and economic disparity amplified by technological change is a major risk factor during this transition.
context: Responded to the moderator's closing question. Acknowledged that technological advancements may deliver benefits over time but warned of a period of dislocation.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Audience Member 1
extraction: The Board of Peace charter issued by the White House goes beyond the mandate of UNSC Resolution 2803 because it omits any reference to Gaza and instead claims authority to solve global problems.
context: Researcher at Princeton University. Directly challenged Waltz on the gap between the UNSC resolution text and the White House charter. [word uncertain in source]

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Audience Member 3
extraction: The Board of Peace cannot credibly claim to be a peace mechanism when it includes Israel's Prime Minister but excludes any Palestinian representation, and Israel is already violating the ceasefire 1,500 times while Palestinians are held accountable three times a day.
context: Mustafa Barghouti from Palestine. Directly challenged the structure and legitimacy of the Board of Peace.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
speaker: Zanny Minton Beddoes
extraction: The panel itself reproduced the power imbalance it was supposed to analyze — the conversation was wholly dominated by the US and EU blocs while middle powers (Saudi Arabia, Colombia) were squeezed to the margins.
context: Moderator's self-critical observation near the end: 'I have completely failed to instill a rules-based order here, because this conversation has been wholly dominated by the two biggest economic and political blocs.' Acknowledged she couldn't get to many audience questions.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extraction: Does the United States still believe in a multilateral system where rules would constrain its own actions, or only in multilateral arrangements it controls? The moderator and an audience member asked this directly; Waltz answered with examples of results-oriented engagement but never affirmed that the US accepts constraints on itself.
context: Moderator asked explicitly at turn 26: 'does the president believe in a multilateral system where there are rules that would constrain the United States or not?' Waltz responded with focused multilateralism examples. Audience Member 2 from Poland asked similarly. The gap remained open.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extraction: How can accountability for violations of international law be enforced against powerful states in any reformed order? Kallas raised this as the central deficit; the Palestinian audience member gave a concrete case; no one proposed a workable mechanism.
context: Kallas repeatedly argued for accountability as the missing element. Barghouti asked who would hold Israel accountable. Waltz framed the issue as results-focused. No panelist offered a structural answer to how enforcement would work.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extraction: Can 'focused multilateralism' — results-oriented coalitions assembled by a dominant power — deliver the equality of states and rule-of-law protections that universal institutions were designed to provide, or does it inherently entrench great-power prerogative?
context: Emerged from the tension between Waltz's advocacy for focused multilateralism delivering peace in Gaza and the Caucasus, and Kallas's insistence that any reformed system must preserve equality of states and not let one person make all decisions. Neither side resolved the tension.

session: Breaking Point: The International Order Between Reform and Destruction
extraction: How should burden-sharing in a reformed multilateral system be structured so that the largest funder's demands for reform are compatible with the equality of states that smaller countries need to survive?
context: Waltz argued the US pays more than 180 countries combined and deserves proportional influence over reform; Kallas argued that equality of states is non-negotiable and that Europe's development-aid contributions are substantial. The panel ended without reconciling these positions.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez
extraction: The Democratic Party betrayed its working-class base by dismissing populist demands like a $15 minimum wage, tuition-free public college, and expanded healthcare as too extreme, even while claiming to listen to working people.
context: Recounted a closed-door meeting of senior Democrats after her 2018 election where party leaders derided the platform she had campaigned on as a waitress who couldn't afford rent or health insurance.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez
extraction: The party seen as most betraying the working class tends to be whichever party is currently governing; the political pendulum in the US now swings against the Republican majority for the same reasons it once swung against Democrats.
context: Framed as part of an 8-year trajectory of growing recognition of past errors including the Iraq War and NAFTA.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez
extraction: A working-class-centered politics is necessary both to succeed electorally and to stave off authoritarianism, which uses scapegoating to channel frustration over economic inequality.
context: Positioned authoritarianism as offering 'siren calls' that redirect legitimate economic grievances toward blaming scapegoats.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Manfred Weber
extraction: The EPP maintains a clear firewall against cooperation with parties that are not pro-Europe, pro-Ukraine, and pro-rule of law, and he personally expelled Viktor Orbán from the EPP for violating these principles.
context: Referenced expelling Orbán and cited Donald Tusk fighting against Kaczyński and Konfederacja in Poland as the EPP's DNA. Explicitly rejected J.D. Vance's 2025 MSC claim that such firewalls are undemocratic.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Manfred Weber
extraction: The center-left in Europe bears significant responsibility for feeding populism by refusing to support strict migration enforcement measures proposed by the European Commission, which are centrist rather than radical-right positions.
context: Cited a European Parliament vote on a safe third country concept where socialists declined to support implementation of rules agreed by member states.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Manfred Weber
extraction: The center-right can win back populist voters by demonstrating concrete results — such as reducing illegal migration by 80% and delivering trade deals like Mercosur — rather than by cooperating with the far right.
context: Directly addressed the hypothetical voter 'Guido' and asked whether he really believes AfD claims, citing cooperation with Giorgia Meloni on Mediterranean migration.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Daiana Fernández Molero
extraction: Milei's program is not anarcho-capitalism or populism but 'capitalism of rules' — replacing crony capitalism with genuine market competition through free trade, antitrust, and budget discipline, which is the opposite of the short-termist economic populism Latin America has repeatedly tried.
context: Distinguished economic populism (short-term spending, price controls, wealth taxes that destroy wealth) from political populism (denying pluralism, eroding institutions), and argued Milei is neither. Referenced Dornbusch and Müller's framework. [word uncertain in source]

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Daiana Fernández Molero
extraction: When discussing populism, we must distinguish between style and substance — an uncomfortable political style does not make someone a populist.
context: Offered as a corrective to the framing that Milei's chainsaw rhetoric and persona resemble Musk/DOGE-style populism.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Petr Pavel
extraction: People in former communist countries expected all the benefits of capitalism while retaining all the benefits of socialism — no responsibility — and the resulting frustration when this proved impossible created fertile ground for populists who fed on insecurity.
context: Offered as a personal perception rather than a thorough analysis of why populism thrives in post-communist Central and Eastern Europe.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Petr Pavel
extraction: Populism should not automatically be labeled as something bad; listening to people's frustrations and translating them into policy is the very meaning of politics — the problem begins only when populism is turned against the institutions that protect democracy.
context: Noted that some politicians are proud to be called populists because they see responsiveness to public grievance as core to their democratic mandate.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Petr Pavel
extraction: The best protection against populist leaders in power is a strong system of checks and balances plus a vibrant civil society, because populist politicians once in office moderate their extreme positions under the weight of governing responsibility and remain sensitive to public opinion.
context: Offered in response to the scenario of Bardella as French president and Farage as UK prime minister — both nuclear powers led by far-right nationalists.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez
extraction: Extreme income inequality is a direct driver of social instability, authoritarianism, and right-wing populism, and should therefore be treated as a security issue alongside defense spending.
context: Argued that failure to deliver higher wages and rein in monopolies allows corporate power to rival that of nation-states, with billionaires throwing their weight around in domestic and global politics.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez
extraction: Corporate consolidation can reach a point where a company's power rivals that of nation-states, undermining democratic governance — antitrust enforcement is therefore a foundational democratic value, not just an economic one.
context: Connected US antitrust tradition to the current moment of billionaires influencing domestic and global politics.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Manfred Weber
extraction: Fighting populism requires not just delivering on citizens' material concerns but also showing brave, visionary leadership that dominates the public discourse rather than following public opinion — as Kohl did with the euro despite its deep unpopularity in Germany.
context: Cited Adenauer's reconciliation with France seven years after WWII and Kohl's introduction of the euro as examples of leaders who fought for unpopular ideas and won public trust. Also advocated for a directly elected EU president and a European army.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Manfred Weber
extraction: The biggest threat to future democracies is that social media has destroyed the shared fact base required for democratic discourse and has made compromise — the core principle of democracy — unrewarded and invisible.
context: Argued that without agreement on facts, competing policy offers from socialists, greens, liberals, or Christian Democrats cannot be meaningfully debated; noted that compromise gets no traction on social media where only black-and-white positions are amplified.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Katrin Bennhold
extraction: Despite passing the DMA and DSA, European leaders have not pushed hard enough to regulate social media algorithms, which represents a failure of democracy itself.
context: Challenged Weber after he cited the DMA and DSA as proof of action, pressing on why more hasn't been done.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Daiana Fernández Molero
extraction: Increased US involvement in Latin America is great news for the region because it creates competitive pressure on Europe to finalize trade deals, fills a vacuum previously occupied by authoritarian powers hostile to market economy and democracy, and brings more trade and investment.
context: Cited Blinken's visit to Argentina when Milei was elected and a new US-Argentina trade agreement awaiting congressional ratification.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Daiana Fernández Molero
extraction: Venezuelan exiles welcomed US intervention in Venezuela because sovereignty for them was not about oil — they had already lost their country by being forced into exile, and it returned hope after multilateral organizations failed to produce concrete results.
context: Responded to moderator's challenge about a 'naked grab for oil in Venezuela,' drawing on personal contact with Venezuelan exiles in Argentina.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez
extraction: In a rules-based order, hypocrisy is vulnerability — the West's habit of looking the other way for inconvenient populations undermines the legitimacy of the entire system and threatens democracies globally.
context: Listed examples: kidnapping a foreign head of state, threatening allies to colonize Greenland, and looking the other way in a genocide. Framed the large Democratic presence at MSC as signaling readiness for a new chapter that eliminates these hypocrisies.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez
extraction: Trump's foreign policy aims to withdraw the US from the world so that authoritarians can carve out geographic domains — Trump commanding the Western Hemisphere, Putin bullying Europe — and global alliances like the Trans-Pacific Partnership are the hard stop against this authoritarian consolidation.
context: Responded to a question about which institutions Democrats would want to save; mentioned USAID, dozens of global compacts, and the TPP.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Manfred Weber
extraction: Germany already spends 40% of GDP on social issues, so the left can no longer win votes by simply adding social benefits; substantive structural reforms to the social model are needed, and these will be hard to sell against populists.
context: Contrasted the German and American situations; argued the German social system is 'perfectly organised' but unsustainable given demographics, requiring creativity beyond spending more money.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Petr Pavel
extraction: For the Czech Republic, defense spending and social spending are not in contradiction: a stronger defense industry means more tax revenue for social benefits, and the EU's provision excluding defense spending above 2% from deficit calculations encourages this dual approach.
context: Responded to the audience question from Greece about whether defense and social equality policies can coexist.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Daiana Fernández Molero
extraction: Regulation can be harmful if done poorly or prematurely — Argentina's experience with housing price controls that backfired shows the danger, and attempting to regulate AI without understanding it could be similarly damaging.
context: Pushed back against the moderator's and other panelists' calls for more regulation, citing a tendency in Argentine Congress for politicians to pass new laws to gain attention rather than to solve problems.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Daiana Fernández Molero
extraction: Large social spending alone does not fight inequality or address voters' real grievances — Argentina spent heavily on social benefits and it didn't work; what matters is spending correctly, with transparency and evaluation of whether policies actually deliver.
context: Offered as a counterpoint to the assumption that defense-versus-social is the key trade-off; argued the quality of spending matters more than the quantity.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Katrin Bennhold
extraction: Reframed the defense spending debate as a question of democratic resilience: if rising defense budgets crowd out public services, democracies may be undermining their internal security by failing to provide the social conditions that sustain public trust.
context: Cited France's discovery that annual debt interest payments now exceed its entire education budget; prompted by Petr Pavel's comments about checks and balances.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
extraction: Can democracies simultaneously increase defense spending and deliver the material gains for working people that are necessary to stave off populism, or is there an inescapable trade-off?
context: Emerged from the exchange between Bennhold (defense crowds out services), Ocasio-Cortez (inequality is a security issue), Weber (you can only spend a euro once), Pavel (defense industry can fund social benefits), and Fernández Molero (spending more isn't enough — spending correctly matters). No consensus reached.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
extraction: Is there a meaningful distinction between responsive populism (listening to and translating citizens' frustrations into policy) and dangerous populism (turning grievances against democratic institutions), and if so, where is the line?
context: Pavel argued populism is not inherently bad and that many politicians proudly claim the label; Fernández Molero distinguished economic from political populism; Ocasio-Cortez linked populist energy to working-class centering; but no panelist converged on criteria for when populism crosses into democratic threat.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
extraction: How should democracies regulate social media algorithms to restore a shared fact base and reward compromise, given that existing measures like the DMA and DSA are seen as insufficient and premature regulation risks doing harm?
context: Tension between Weber (social media destroying democratic discourse), Bennhold (nobody is pushing hard enough to regulate), and Fernández Molero (be careful with regulation — it can be harmful if done without understanding). No resolution offered.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
extraction: As the EPP maintains its firewall against the far right but occasionally votes with far-right parties on specific issues like migration, how long can this distinction hold before the firewall becomes functionally meaningless?
context: Moderator pressed Weber on voting with the AfD and on whether coalition government with them is inevitable given the AfD is Germany's second-largest party. Weber insisted the firewall is firm but argued the center must adopt strict migration enforcement to deny the far right its issue.

session: Vox Populi? Responding to the Rise of Populism
speaker: Audience Member 3
extraction: The core politics required to defeat authoritarian populists must include promising national renewal by de-rigging the marketplace, rebuilding an optimistic opportunity economy, and strengthening social capital — not imitating strongman leadership.
context: Self-identified as Liam Byrne, UK Labour MP; argued populists always use a formula of civilizational decline, nostalgia, and emergency that demands a strongman. [word uncertain in source]

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Gladden J. Pappin
extraction: The NATO treaty's core commitments to liberty, common heritage, and civilization have come under threat, but the threat has been wrongly perceived as coming primarily from conservative, right-wing, and nationally-oriented parties rather than from progressive overreach.
context: Cited the preamble to the NATO treaty as his framing device, arguing that the Trump administration's understanding of the alliance core should be taken seriously.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Radosław Sikorski
extraction: There is a genuine civilizational difference between the US and Europe on freedom of speech: Europe believes in freedom of speech with responsibility, and the US (via its vice president at MSC the prior year) telling Europeans their conception of free speech is censorship is unacceptable imposition of values.
context: Referenced Poland's laws forbidding advocacy of fascism and communism as legitimate, historically grounded limits on speech, contrasting them with near-absolute US First Amendment norms.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Hillary R. Clinton
extraction: The intra-Western debate about values is almost healthy — the problem is not disagreement itself but when it is conducted through intimidation, bullying, and cancellation, and when populist nationalism poses challenges to post-war consensus without democratic accountability.
context: Characterized the rise of muscular populist nationalism as distinct from conservatism, framing the core threat as the method of contestation rather than the existence of competing views.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Petr Macinka
extraction: The West is no longer a unified civilization but a contested territory of values, divided between a conservative vision rooted in national sovereignty and tradition, and a progressive vision of supranational social engineering, with the gap widening.
context: Framed the divide as conservatism vs. progressivism, with progressivism rejecting traditions and imposing artificial social constructs.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Radosław Sikorski
extraction: Globalization was not a progressive project — it was a business idea about maximizing profits, supported for decades by Republican businessmen who wanted cheap labor, and tinged with the racism of exporting dirty jobs to poorer countries.
context: Direct pushback on Macinka's framing of globalization as progressive ideology. Pappin conceded the point ('He's right').

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Ivan Krastev
extraction: The problem is not that the West doesn't exist but that we have too many Wests — the Cold War West (the free world), the West as civilization (who's in and who's out), and others — and the defining characteristic of the West since the mid-19th century has always been its pluralistic, self-questioning nature.
context: Referenced a book by a Greek scholar at the London School of Economics ('The West: A History of an Idea') and a Soviet-era joke about being told 'we are them' to illustrate the multiplicity of Western self-definitions.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Gladden J. Pappin
extraction: After the Cold War, progressive commitments were imported into the victorious system, decision-making shifted from national governments to transnational bodies, and conservative voices were systematically excluded from elite institutions and debates.
context: Argued this exclusion was a shared view among conservative and nationally-oriented parties on both sides of the Atlantic.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Gladden J. Pappin
extraction: The post-Cold War progressive consensus wore down the family, borders, and industrial strength — fundamental sources of Western power — to the point of injuring the West's ability to maintain its own NATO commitments.
context: Extension of his argument about exclusion of conservative voices; framed the erosion not as a culture-war complaint but as a strategic vulnerability.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Radosław Sikorski
extraction: Mass migration was not a left-wing project; it was supported for decades by Republican businessmen wanting cheap Latin American labor in the US, and in Europe the desire for economic growth through migration cut across left and right.
context: Direct rebuttal to Pappin's framing of migration as a progressive-driven erosion. Pappin explicitly agreed ('He's right').

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Radosław Sikorski
extraction: The EU is democratically governed — the European Parliament is elected, the Commission is nominated by elected governments — and the myth that Brussels bureaucrats impose directives on member states is complete nonsense that gave us Brexit.
context: Pushed back on the conservative-Eurosceptic claim that EU governance lacks democratic legitimacy; later debated this point directly with Macinka.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Hillary R. Clinton
extraction: The expansion of freedom to include Black people, women, and gay people — including same-sex marriage, which she characterized as creating a family — was a culmination of Western values, not a progressive distortion of them.
context: Directly responded to the conservative framing of post-war social change as erosion, arguing the expansion of rights was the realization, not betrayal, of the West's founding commitments.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Hillary R. Clinton
extraction: The populist-nationalist movement is at its core about controlling people — who they are, how they look, who they love — and its nostalgia for 'making America great again' recalls a past that was open and welcoming only to white men and capitalist enterprise.
context: Framed the conservative-progressive divide not as a genuine values debate but as a power grab disguised in civilizational language.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Hillary R. Clinton
extraction: The Trump administration's position toward Ukraine is disgraceful: forcing Ukraine into a surrender deal with Putin is shameful, and Trump and Putin profiting from Ukrainian misery is historically corrupt.
context: Called Ukraine the front line of Western democracy and freedom, arguing Trump either doesn't understand or doesn't care about the suffering.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Hillary R. Clinton
extraction: Trump has betrayed the West, the NATO Charter, the Atlantic Charter, and the Universal Declaration of Human Rights; he is modeling himself after Putin's unaccountable exercise of power.
context: Went beyond criticizing policy to characterize Trump's approach as a betrayal of the entire postwar normative architecture.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Petr Macinka
extraction: Trump is a reaction to policies that went too far from regular people and reality — cancel culture, the woke revolution, gender ideology, and climate alarmism — not an unprovoked attack on Western values.
context: Offered as direct response to Clinton's characterization of Trump; triggered a sharp exchange when Clinton interjected asking 'Which gender? Women having their rights?'

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Petr Macinka
extraction: Ukraine fights first for Ukraine's own future, freedom, and sovereignty — not 'our' freedom — and only the United States currently has the geopolitical power to force Russia to stop the war, so the priority should be a ceasefire however achieved.
context: Pushed back on Clinton's framing of Ukraine as fighting for Western democracy as such, while arguing pragmatically that only US leverage matters.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Ivan Krastev
extraction: The way the current US administration defines freedom of speech is really about the rights of American technology companies, not about conservative values; a genuine conservative or nationalist should not accept foreign tech platforms addressing their citizens with AI-driven content.
context: Directed at Pappin's defense of the conservative free-speech position, arguing it collapses into corporate interest rather than national sovereignty.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Ivan Krastev
extraction: You cannot achieve peace in Ukraine by totally bracketing the question of moral responsibility — who started the war matters, and peace that destroys the moral integrity of a nation is not real peace.
context: Told the Versailles Treaty anecdote: 'Nobody is going to claim that it was Belgium that invaded Germany.' Argued peace requires more than security guarantees.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Ivan Krastev
extraction: Being highly nationalistic triggers the nationalism of others, which is why far-right parties in Europe strongly opposed Trump on Greenland — for them, land is not real estate — exposing a major contradiction in the conservative-nationalist position.
context: Pointed to European far-right opposition to Greenland annexation as evidence that nationalism is self-undermining when projected outward.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Radosław Sikorski
extraction: True conservatism — Burke, Churchill, Scruton, Thatcher, Buckley — would not approve of what is happening now; the Trump/Orbán strain is not genuine conservatism.
context: Listed conservative intellectual and political figures he personally knew or admired, asserting ownership of the conservative tradition against the panel's national-populist voices.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Gladden J. Pappin
extraction: There is no plan for a successful ending of the Ukraine war on the battlefield; the war will not be won militarily, and those who opposed Trump's call for negotiations merely tried to box him as a Putin apologist.
context: Challenged what he characterized as the absence of any alternative strategy from critics of Trump's negotiation push.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Hillary R. Clinton
extraction: There is a plan for Ukraine: give them Tomahawk missiles, more Patriot defense systems, and allow them to strike targets inside Russia — inflicting enough pain on Russia is what brought Putin to the negotiating table and would create conditions for a better deal.
context: Direct rebuttal to Pappin's claim that no military-path plan exists; cited Ukraine's earlier cross-border strikes as evidence the strategy works.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Ivan Krastev
extraction: There is something worse than liberal hypocrisy, and that is a world with no hypocrisy at all — a world where only power matters — because then small countries have no reason to distinguish between the US and China, and American exceptionalism is reduced to being exceptionally powerful.
context: Distinguished between Carney's critique (treating Ukraine and Gaza differently) and Trump's critique (the biggest hypocrisy is pretending countries are equal). Argued the fiction of sovereign equality, while hypocritical, is necessary for any international order. Referenced his multi-country research project on hypocrisy.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Audience Member 3
extraction: The panel's framing of conservative vs. liberal is a false division: Trump is not a conservative but a demagogue and self-enricher who doesn't operate on principles, while genuine conservatives in the GOP are too frightened to speak; the real dynamic is demagoguery and populism against confusion on both sides.
context: Argued that economic causes (outsourcing jobs to China) destroyed prospects for huge segments of the population, which had nothing to do with culture-war issues like gender.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Gladden J. Pappin
extraction: Conservatism has changed because both center-left social progressivism and center-right free-market orthodoxy didn't know where to stop, creating a bipartisan 'conspiracy of the center' that sold out the country in the 1990s and 2000s; in Europe, Orbán was right 10 years ago on migration, family, and peace, and critics will eventually have to acknowledge it.
context: Identified himself as a citizen of the Osage Tribal Nation who voted for Trump. Conceded that postwar progressive advances (including Native American suffrage) were important but argued the progressive impulse exceeded its mandate.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Hillary R. Clinton
extraction: America was founded as a creed, an idea, and a philosophy rooted in the founders' understanding of liberal thought; JD Vance's redefinition of America as 'a particular place with a particular people' is historically wrong, politically dangerous, and should be rejected whole cloth.
context: Referenced the 13 colonies and the founding documents, and sarcastically noted she didn't know Vance was 'such a firm advocate for Native Americans' given his blood-and-soil rhetoric. Responded to audience question quoting Vance directly.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Gladden J. Pappin
extraction: As a member of the Osage Tribal Nation, Vance has not used the phrase 'blood and soil,' and Clinton's characterization is not accurate to the thought going on in the administration.
context: Rebutted Clinton's framing by denying the specific charge while not contesting the broader redefinition of American identity. [word uncertain in source]

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Radosław Sikorski
extraction: There was an unspoken agreement among Western allies not to interfere in each other's party-political rivalries; the Trump administration's receiving of Poland's populist presidential candidate and funding of MAGA-aligned think tanks in Europe breaks this norm, and once the US picks sides in allied countries' politics, a different and destructive dynamic begins.
context: Noted Poland was for decades the most pro-American country in Europe, with government and opposition competing to be more pro-American; US interference risks destroying that consensus.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Petr Macinka
extraction: The sovereign nation-state is the only platform that can guarantee democracy because it is the only way to maintain cultural continuity and keep power within limited, accountable institutions; democracy cannot fully exist at the supranational level.
context: Debated directly with Sikorski about the EU's democratic credentials, arguing the European Parliament lacks legislative initiative and Czech voters have no influence over who becomes another country's Commissioner.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Petr Macinka
extraction: The European Parliament is the only parliament in the world that cannot initiate legislation, which fundamentally undermines the claim that the EU is democratically governed in the same way as a nation-state.
context: Part of extended exchange with Sikorski on EU legitimacy. Sikorski countered that legislation is brought forward by the Council and Commission, both accountable to elected bodies.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Ivan Krastev
extraction: AI is going to replace more people than migrants — 'the other great replacement' — and will force a redefinition of what a nation, a civilization, and the West are, making the current migration-centered culture war a sideshow compared to what's coming.
context: Raised in closing remarks alongside the observation that national mortality — whether anyone will speak Bulgarian in 100 years — is a more existential threat than any current policy debate.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Ivan Krastev
extraction: In the European tradition, civilization was always universal and singular, not plural; redefining it as a clash between civilizations is incoherent in a continent of nation-states speaking different languages — this is not India, China, or the United States.
context: Responded to the audience question about 'values' vs. 'civilization' framing, noting Gandhi's quip that European civilization was 'a good idea.'

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Ivan Krastev
extraction: If Trump's civilizational framework were applied consistently, Denmark — tough on migration, socially cohesive, economically competitive — is the last country whose territory he should be trying to seize, exposing the incoherence of using civilizational rhetoric to justify power plays.
context: Final point before session close; used Denmark/Greenland as a reductio ad absurdum of the civilizational argument.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Gladden J. Pappin
extraction: The rules-based international order was always underwritten by power, and recognizing that the US needs to restore its own strength does not mean rejecting all international norms — but rules won't enforce themselves without strong countries behind them.
context: Made in response to moderator's challenge about Greenland and international law, arguing the administration's moves reflect a realist correction, not lawlessness.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Petr Macinka
extraction: Western societies should stop labeling ideological opponents as fascists or Nazis; this practice is dividing society and making constructive dialogue impossible, when what is needed is learning to listen to each other again.
context: Responded to the heated exchanges on the panel, arguing the labeling itself had gone too far. Sikorski and Clinton pushed back that actual fascists exist in Europe.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Gladden J. Pappin
extraction: Conservative political parties have been systematically excluded from public debate in Europe, including by legacy media networks, and this exclusion — not just American tech platforms — is the real freedom-of-speech crisis.
context: Made while defending Trump's social media presence and noting gratitude to MSC for hosting the debate; Sikorski interjected that European states rightly exclude fascists.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Hillary R. Clinton
extraction: Migration went too far and has been disruptive and destabilizing, but it needs to be fixed in a humane way — with secure borders that don't torture and kill people — and more deportations were carried out under Clinton and Obama administrations than under Trump's first term.
context: Conceded ground on migration as a legitimate concern while challenging the narrative that Trump's approach is more effective or principled.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
extraction: Is there a left-wing critique of the rules-based international order that converges with the right-wing critique — and if so, what does that convergence mean for the future of the West?
context: Audience Member 1 cited AOC's statement that 'our exceptions became our rules' and asked the panel to engage the left critique of the rules-based order; the panel did not address it.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
extraction: Does Western hypocrisy on Gaza versus Ukraine — the charge raised by much of the non-Western world — constitute a fatal blow to the coherence of 'the West' as a values-based concept?
context: The moderator explicitly raised Gaza as a test of Western values consistency and asked for responses; the panel largely deflected, with Sikorski offering only irony and Clinton and Pappin returning to Ukraine instead.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
extraction: Is the conservative-progressive framing of the West-West divide the right one, or is Trump/Trumpism something categorically different from conservatism — and if so, what is it?
context: Tension between Macinka and Pappin (who framed the divide as conservative vs. progressive), Sikorski (who claimed true conservatism would not approve of Trump), Audience Member 3 (who called Trump a demagogue, not a conservative), and Clinton (who called it populist nationalism). The room never converged.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
extraction: Can peace in Ukraine be achieved through negotiation without establishing moral responsibility for the war, or does any deal that brackets responsibility fatally undermine the principle of territorial sovereignty on which the Western order depends?
context: Emerged from the tension between Krastev's insistence that peace without moral integrity is not real peace, Pappin's argument that there is no battlefield alternative to negotiation, and Clinton's claim that arming Ukraine more aggressively creates negotiating leverage. No resolution.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Audience Member 1
extraction: The critique of the rules-based order is not only coming from the right; AOC's formulation that 'we called out exceptions to our values and our rules, and eventually our exceptions became our own rules' suggests a left-wing critique that may overlap with the right's, which the panel should engage.
context: Referenced AOC's statement at an MSC event the previous evening. The panel did not pick up this reframing.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
extraction: What are the implications of the US administration funding far-right and MAGA-aligned think tanks in Europe, and does this break the unspoken norm against allies interfering in each other's domestic party politics?
context: Audience Member 4 (British MP) raised the $200 million figure; Sikorski called it 'completely outrageous' and warned it would destroy pro-American consensus. The moderator flagged it would come back up the agenda but the panel ran out of time.

session: The West-West Divide: What Remains of Common Values
speaker: Audience Member 6
extraction: The distinction between 'Western values' (used by the Biden administration) and 'Western civilization' (used 12 times by Rubio at MSC) is a telling shift in discourse: the panel should clarify which concept they are defending.
context: Noted that Rubio's speech that morning used 'civilization' 12 times; this values-vs-civilization distinction was partially picked up by Krastev in closing remarks but not fully explored.
